title: Component Implementations and Performance
breadcrumbs: Development Considerations for Performance and Scalability
Performance Guidelines for Component Logic and Implementation
text: Component Implementations and Performance Your choice of implementation technology and the way in which the component is implemented can affect performance. 3GL Components Some functionality might require an implementation tool that uses processing capacity more efficiently than the tool currently applied, even if its productivity is less. For example, components for mathematical calculations that are built in 3GL often have better performance than those implemented in 4GL. For more information, see C, C++, and Other 3GLs . Stored Procedures Most DBMSs support the use of stored procedures, which are parsed pieces of code that are stored in a database. Programs that have access to the database invoke the stored procedures, either explicitly or implicitly, via database triggers. The invoked software is processed on the database server. Stored procedures can enhance performance because they can usually do a join or perform a complex query faster than Uniface. For example, all complex calculations required for a query could be performed within a DBMS stored procedure, and only the result of the calculation would be sent to the client. The disadvantages of using stored procedures are: Portability of the application is reduced by tying it to a specific DBMS You cannot use hitlists or overflow tables, which are relevant to a Uniface implementation, but not to a database implementation. Session and Entity Services Session services and entity services separate business logic from the user interface and from the data access tier. Session services handle complex business rules affecting multiple entities. Entity services are optional and handle simple business rules. Session services and entity services can run on different processors than the forms and DBMS and thus are a means for balancing the system load. A 3-tier architecture implemented with session services or entity services increases the system load because each service is an extra process requiring extra communication between the presentation component and the service. This is especially true when reporting data entry errors, for example, syntax or business rule violations. By default, data validation is performed on the session service after a store instruction. To perform earlier validation, the following options are available: Force data to be stored every time the form is modified Explicitly activate an operation that is responsible for all validation on the entity service, whenever appropriate. Explicitly propagate occurrence modifications to the entity service by activating the uupdate operation in the appropriate triggers on the form. An entity service is dedicated to a specific business object. Many presentation components can use the same data retrieved by a single entity service. A component that retrieves data via an entity service has access to the data that has already been retrieved by the service for other components. If necessary, the entity service retrieves additional data using the retrieve/a instruction, without removing existing data from the entity service. Re-use data retrieved from the database to decrease the system load. Services for Data Conversion and Reports Allow only one occurrence of each entity in reports or services that are used exclusively for data conversion. Performance decreases if more than one occurrence is painted on such components. Use the C (clear) option with the print instruction to discard occurrences that have already been printed from memory. Use of Images Using large images, especially bitmaps, can slow down the application because of the time needed to build up such images. Images that are part of the application graphics, such as icons, logos, and illustrations, should be defined as glyphs. At run time, glyphs can be placed in a UAR file. Image fields in entities, such as pictures of employees, can be stored in the DBMS because Uniface supports the Binary Large OBject (BLOB) format. Related Links Service Stored Procedures (SSPs) Uniface supports the execution of DBMS stored procedures by using the activate statement to address a Stored Procedure Component defined with the Signature Editor. This concept is referred to within Uniface as Service Stored Procedures. Naming Specifications for Service Store Procedures For Service Stored Procedures, the signature of the Stored Procedure Component maps the names of Uniface components, operations, and parameters to the name of a package, procedure, and parameter names in the procedure. C, C++, and Other 3GLs Uniface provides an API that makes it possible to integrate Uniface applications and programs created in 3GL programming languages such as C, C++, Visual Basic, Cobol, Fortran, and Pascal.
